description:
You are acting as a deployment-engineer who must roll out a long-running helper script that is already present on the system.  
    Your goal is to (1) launch the script so it survives logout, (2) record its PID and a timestamp, and (3) take a one-off snapshot of the new process’s CPU / RAM usage.

    System layout that you will find when the session starts  
    ---------------------------------------------------------
    • Directory: /home/user/deployment  
    • File:      /home/user/deployment/deploy_app.sh  (already executable)  

    Required end-state  
    ------------------  
    After you finish, the system **must** satisfy all five checks below.  
    1. The script /home/user/deployment/deploy_app.sh is running as exactly **one** background process that is *detached* from the current shell (i.e. it will keep running after logout).  
    2. The PID of that background process is written to the file /home/user/deployment/deployment.pid in the exact two-line key-value format shown here (no leading or trailing spaces):  
       PID=<integer>  
       STARTED_AT=<UTC-timestamp>  
       where the timestamp is generated with the command `date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`.  
    3. A one-line process snapshot is saved to /home/user/deployment/process_snapshot.log.  
       The line **must** contain the three whitespace-separated fields in the following order, with no extra text:  
           <PID> <CPU_PERCENT> <MEM_PERCENT>  
       Example (your numbers will differ):  
           12345 0.0 0.1  
       Field rules:  
         • <PID> is the integer you just stored in deployment.pid.  
         • <CPU_PERCENT> is the %CPU value reported by `ps` **without a trailing “%”**.  
         • <MEM_PERCENT> is the %MEM value reported by `ps` **without a trailing “%”**.  
    4. The snapshot file contains exactly one line (no header, no blank lines).  
    5. The deployment.pid and process_snapshot.log files are both world-readable (chmod 644 or more open).  

    Constraints  
    -----------  
    • Accomplish everything with a simple series of 3–4 terminal commands.  
    • Do NOT modify deploy_app.sh itself.  
    • You do NOT need super-user privileges.  
    • No additional files, directories, or background jobs should be created other than those explicitly mentioned.  

    Important: double-check the exact file names, locations, and output formats above; the automated grader parses them literally.
truth:
Initial filesystem state (before the agent starts)  
    --------------------------------------------------  
    1. The directory /home/user/deployment already exists and is writable by user “user”.  
    2. /home/user/deployment/deploy_app.sh exists with mode 755 and the exact contents:  
       #!/bin/bash
       while true; do
           echo "Deploying..." >> /home/user/deployment/app.log
           sleep 60
       done
    3. No deployment.pid or process_snapshot.log files exist yet.  
    4. No running instances of deploy_app.sh exist.  

    Verification expectations (after the agent finishes)  
    ----------------------------------------------------  
    • One (and only one) running process has the command line `/bin/bash /home/user/deployment/deploy_app.sh` (path may appear truncated in `ps` output, which is acceptable).  
    • The PID for that process is referred to below as $PID.  
    • /home/user/deployment/deployment.pid exists, mode 0644, with exactly two lines separated by a single newline character and ending with a trailing newline:  
         PID=$PID\n
         STARTED_AT=YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ\n  
      where the timestamp string is valid UTC ISO-8601 as produced by `date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`.  
    • /home/user/deployment/process_snapshot.log exists, mode 0644, containing exactly the string:  
         "$PID $CPU $MEM\n"  
      where $CPU and $MEM are floating-point numbers that match the values emitted by  
           ps -p $PID -o pid=,%cpu=,%mem=  
      The PID field must match the one in deployment.pid.  
    • No other new files are present inside /home/user/deployment, and no additional deploy_app.sh instances are running.
